ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Vada Pav exists; closest defensible match is 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_03311737) as Vada Pav is a filled bread roll. A more specific Indian street food class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Bread rolls and buns) is the closest defensible anchor for the pav component; no specific Vada Pav composite dish code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vada Pav or equivalent Indian street food composite dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Vada Pav is approximately 150 g, comprising one pav (bread roll) filled with one batata vada and accompanied by chutneys and a fried green chili.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Vada Pav are derived from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for boiled potato, gram flour, white bread, and cooking oil, combined to reflect the standard Vada Pav preparation. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json record (confidence 0.90–0.95). Cholesterol confirmed at 0 mg per ICMR-NIN and HPB data, consistent with a plant-based deep-fried snack using vegetable oil.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Vada Pav exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from analogous components: white bread roll (GI ~70–75, Atkinson et al. 2008) and boiled/fried potato (GI ~65–85). The composite GI of 70 reflects the dominant carbohydrate contributors (white bread and potato starch) and is consistent with the ICMR-NIN assessment. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 39.75 g) / 100 ≈ 27.8; rounded to 24.9 using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment (26.5 − 2.2 = 24.3 g per 100 g × 1.5 serving factor = 36.45 g; GL = 70 × 35.6 / 100 ≈ 24.9).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; ~570 mg per 150 g serving) from added salt, chutneys, and seasoned batter, combined with refined carbohydrates and deep-fried fat.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Vada Pav to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid high-sodium chutneys to keep per-serving sodium below 400 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) driven by white bread roll and fried potato starch, delivering approximately 24–28 g of rapidly available carbohydrate per 150 g serving, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Vada Pav; if consumed, pair with a high-fibre side, choose a smaller portion, and monitor postprandial glucose response closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes approximately 9.5 g total fat per 100 g; saturated fat is moderate (~1.5 g per 100 g) when vegetable oil is used, but repeated deep-frying may introduce trans fats and oxidised lipids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Vada Pav due to its deep-fried fat content and high sodium; if consumed, ensure it is freshly prepared in non-hydrogenated vegetable oil and limit frequency to once per week.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (potato) and refined wheat (pav) combined with deep-fried fat may contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance to nightshades and refined starches, and limit deep-fried foods as part of an overall anti-inflammatory dietary approach.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vada Pav is the iconic street food of Mumbai and the broader Maharashtra region of India, often described as the 'Indian burger'. It consists of a batata vada — a spiced mashed potato ball seasoned with mustard seeds, turmeric, green chili, garlic, and coriander, coated in a gram flour (besan) batter and deep-fried until golden — sandwiched inside a soft white bread roll known as pav. The snack is served with three essential chutneys: a dry roasted garlic chutney (lasun chutney), a sweet tamarind chutney, and a spicy green coriander-chili chutney. A fried whole green chili is almost always served alongside. The texture contrasts a crisp, lightly spiced batter exterior with a soft, fluffy potato interior and a pillowy bread roll. Nutritionally, Vada Pav provides approximately 210 kcal per 100 g, with 5.5 g protein, 9.5 g fat, and 26.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium is relatively high at 380 mg per 100 g, making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), driven by the white bread roll and potato starch, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without careful portion control. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, but contains gluten. It is a culturally significant, affordable, and filling snack consumed at breakfast, lunch, or any time of day across Maharashtra and increasingly across India.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for the constituent ingredients (potato, gram flour, white bread, vegetable oil, chutneys) rather than a direct whole-dish measurement of Vada Pav in an approved database. The GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on white bread and potato. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high. Saturated fat is estimated at 1.5 g per 100 g based on typical vegetable oil composition used in deep-frying; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and water content are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture data for the composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or HPB databases.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g per 100 g) is estimated from typical vegetable oil (sunflower/groundnut) used in deep-frying; this value is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN for the composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for the Vada Pav composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: Vada Pav is a plant-based dish prepared with vegetable oil; no animal-derived fat is present in the standard preparation, confirmed consistent with ICMR-NIN plant food data and HPB source. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: Vada Pav contains no animal-derived ingredients in its standard preparation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: no significant dietary vitamin D source is present in the standard plant-based preparation. 6. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (GI ~70–75) and fried potato (GI ~65–85) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Vada Pav exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The input record contained three duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving 150g'); deduplicated to a single default serving of 150 g. 8. food_subgroup corrected from 'Baking ingredients' to 'Street food / Snack' to accurately reflect the dish classification.
